title: Chapter 12 — Scalable Data Management
objectives: Define scalable data management and explain elasticity, efficiency, and resilience
Compare databases, object storage, and lakehouse formats for large datasets
Describe distributed systems patterns including HDFS, Cassandra, and Kafka
Apply CAP theorem, sharding, and consistency models to storage design choices
Use dataset versioning tools such as DVC, Pachyderm, Delta Lake, and Iceberg at scale
Explain data lineage techniques, tools, benefits, and cross-platform gaps
Recognize scalable architectures in streaming media, analytics, and healthcare imaging
Identify emerging trends including serverless compute, graph stores, and streaming platforms

parts.title: Introduction to scalable data management
parts.bridge_in: Chapters 9 through 11 covered advanced collection, synthetic companions, and annotation at scale. This chapter asks how organizations store, distribute, and operate datasets when volume and concurrency outgrow single-node workflows. This opening part defines scalable data management, key principles, and the challenges that drive distributed designs.

parts.title: Databases and cloud storage
parts.bridge_in: Principles alone do not pick a store. Teams need concrete database and storage families for web, mobile, IoT, and analytics workloads. This part surveys database types, NoSQL use cases, and cloud object storage across major providers.

parts.title: Formats and storage types
parts.bridge_in: Choosing a database is only half the storage decision. Columnar formats and object versus block media determine how analytics and low-latency services perform. This part covers Parquet, Avro, ORC, and when object or block storage fits.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:12.2 after databases and cloud storage.

parts.title: Distributed systems overview
parts.bridge_in: Single-node disks cannot hold petabyte corpora. Distributed systems partition and replicate data across machines. This part introduces distributed characteristics and concrete examples: HDFS, Cassandra, and Kafka for streams.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:12.2 after formats and storage types.

parts.title: CAP, sharding, and consistency
parts.bridge_in: Distribution introduces trade-offs among consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. This part covers the CAP theorem, sharding and replication patterns, and when strong versus eventual consistency is appropriate.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:12.2 after distributed systems overview.

parts.title: Dataset versioning tools
parts.bridge_in: Chapter 8 covered team-scale documentation and Git-oriented version control. At platform scale, versioning must track large binaries and lakehouse tables. This part surveys DVC remotes, Pachyderm, Delta Lake time travel, and Iceberg snapshots.

parts.title: Versioning benefits and challenges
parts.bridge_in: Tools only help if teams understand why versioning matters and where it fails. This part covers rollback and reproducibility benefits, then storage cost, metadata complexity, and collaboration challenges at scale.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:12.3 after versioning tools.

parts.title: Data lineage tracking
parts.bridge_in: Versioning records what changed; lineage records how data moved and transformed. This part defines provenance, metadata and automated capture techniques, visualization for debugging, and cross-platform lineage gaps.

parts.title: Practical applications and case studies
parts.bridge_in: Storage theory becomes concrete in production architectures. This part walks scalable patterns for streaming media, real-time analytics pipelines, and healthcare imaging at volume.

parts.title: Emerging trends
parts.bridge_in: The chapter closes by looking ahead. This final part surveys serverless compute over object storage, graph and time-series specialty stores, and high-throughput messaging platforms that shape the next generation of scalable pipelines.
